title: Epic: project-agent-orchestration — the long-running loop
body:
Goal: a project-scope agent that knows the whole project, picks up
ready work, delegates to subagents (potentially across providers),
and surfaces what's happening through the multi-client view. End
state: the user files a bug from their phone, the project agent
picks it up, decides whether to work it itself or delegate to a
subagent in a worktree, work progresses, results land back on the
bug, the user sees the outcome.

# Approach

The pattern (drawn from the bigger conversation and from what
Triphammer already does):

- **Project agent** = one long-lived session per project, running on
  the laptop, with the project's tree mounted. Knows about all bugs
  via jjforge. Polls `jjf ready` for next work. Has the project's
  MEMORY.jsonl loaded.
- **Subagents** = ephemeral sessions launched in isolated worktrees,
  scoped to one bug. The `subagent-working-a-git-bug-issue` skill
  (post-cutover: needs renaming to `subagent-working-a-jjforge-issue`)
  enforces the closure protocol. When a subagent finishes, the bug
  has a structured closing comment and a status change.
- **Multi-agent backends.** Subagents don't have to be the same model
  as the project agent. Claude as planner + DeepSeek as executor is
  one shape; Claude + Codex is another. The orchestration layer
  dispatches per bug.
- **Triphammer (in `~/p/research/triphammer/`) is the prior art.**
  Polls a Forgejo kanban board, classifies state, spawns
  Claude/Codex/OpenCode per issue, dashboards it. We want the same
  shape but with jjforge as the tracker and goose (or whatever) as
  the agent backend.

Open questions, big ones:

- **Where does the project agent live?** Long-running CLI process,
  daemon mode, embedded in the multi-client daemon (`jjf serve`)?
  Probably the last, but undecided.
- **Convergence loops.** Triphammer's research doc names this as gap
  #1: when an agent fails, today a human moves the card back. We want
  automatic retry with feedback under a budget cap. Hard problem.
- **External validation.** "Who watches the watchmen" — the agent
  self-verifying its work is a known weak point. Need external
  acceptance tests, hold-out scenarios, something.
- **Goose vs Claude Code as the agent backend.** Both are candidates.
  Goose has the ACP delegation primitives; Claude Code has the
  maturity. Decision deferred to when we actually need it.

This is the most speculative epic. The point of filing it sketched-only
is to keep the shape in view; per-ticket detail should wait until at
least `mvp-cli` and `mvp-sync` ship.

# Child tickets we'll file

- `orch-project-agent-skeleton` — the long-running session shape,
  MEMORY loading, ready-poll loop.
- `orch-subagent-dispatch` — launch a subagent per ready bug, isolate
  working dir, enforce the closure skill.
- `orch-cross-provider-delegation` — Claude plans, DeepSeek executes,
  or similar. Builds on the agent-ergonomics subagent protocol.
- `orch-convergence-loop` — auto-retry with feedback under budget cap.
- `orch-holdout-validation` — external acceptance tests.

# Depends on

- `mvp-cli`, `mvp-storage`, `mvp-sync` — the agent's tools.
- `agent-ergonomics` — `ready`, `remember`, the skill.
- `multi-client` — the daemon the project agent runs in.

# Blocks

- The full vision: "ecosystem view," voice-to-bug pipeline closing
  the loop with the project agent picking it up.

# Status

Placeholder (2026-06-21). Sketched only; details deferred until at
least the MVP epics ship. Filed so the shape is captured. Re-filed
in jjforge 2026-06-22 during the git-bug cutover; the original
ticket (`303e15b` in archived `refs/bugs/*`) had no status comments.
